examples.title: Byzantine Generals Problem: Pseudocode
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Byzantine Generals
INPUT: messages from all generals
OUTPUT: consensus decision (attack or retreat)

BEGIN
 FOR EACH general
 IF general is loyal THEN
 send message to all other generals
 ELSE IF general is traitor THEN
 send conflicting messages to different generals
 END FOR
 IF more than 2/3 of generals agree THEN
 return majority decision
 ELSE
 return failure to reach consensus
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates the Byzantine Generals Problem. Loyal generals send the same message to all other generals, while traitors send conflicting messages. If more than 2/3 of generals agree, the algorithm returns the majority decision; otherwise, it returns failure to reach consensus.

examples.title: Byzantine Generals Problem: Python Implementation
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates the Byzantine Generals Problem. It defines a General class with methods to send and receive messages. The simulation initializes a list of generals, some of whom are traitors, and has them communicate with each other. Finally, it checks for consensus among the generals.

examples.title: Byzantine Generals Problem: C Implementation
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates the Byzantine Generals Problem. It defines a General struct and functions to send and receive messages. The simulation initializes an array of generals, some of whom are traitors, and has them communicate with each other. Finally, it checks for consensus among the generals.
